coredata.dc:title: Smart nano-architectures as potential sensing tools for detecting heavy metal ions in aqueous matrices
coredata.dc:creator: Rasheed, Tahir
Shafi, Sameera
Sher, Farooq
coredata.dc:description: The discharge of heavy metal ions into water resources as a result of human activities has become a global issue. Contamination with heavy metal ions poses a major threat to the environment and human health. Therefore, there is a dire need to probe the presence of heavy metal ions in a more selective, facile, quick, cost-effective and sensitive way. Conventional sensors are being utilized to sense heavy metal ions; however, various challenges and limitations like interference, overlapping of oxidation potential, selectivity and sensitivity are associated with them that limit their in-field applicability. Hence, nanomaterial based chemical sensors have emerged as an alternative substitute and are extensively employed for the detection of heavy metal ions as a potent analytical tool. The incorporation of nanomaterials in sensors increases their sensitivity, selectivity, portability, on-site detection capability and device performance. Nanomaterial based electrodes exhibit enhanced performance because surface of electrode at nano-scale level offers high catalytic potential, large active surface area and high conductivity. Therefore, this review addresses the recent progress on chemical sensors based on different nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), metal nanoparticles, graphene, carbon quantum dots and nanocomposites for sensing heavy metals ions using different sensing approaches. Furthermore, various types of optical sensors such as fluorescence, luminescence and colorimetry sensors have been presented in detail.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Zhi and coworkers [128] made graphene-doped MoS 2 aerogels to support Au/Fe 3 O 4 nano-particles. Fe 3 O 4 nanoparticles bestowed magnetic features to aerogels. The prepared nanohybrid materials facilitate facile reusability and recovery of catalyst. Further, the detection was supported by the addition of gold nanoparticles in an aqueous medium. The formed Hg 2+ ions contained aerogel that stimulated peroxidase mimetic potential. In addition to the per-oxidase mimetic potential, upon introduction of Hg 2+ ions the direct solution color change of the nanocomposites has also been referred to as the detection of Hg 2+ ions. By following a simple one-pot oxidation-reduction reaction, Yan and his colleagues [129] prepared multi-functional graphene gold nanocomposites (G-AuNPs). The formed G-AuNPs initiate a colorimetric change upon introduction of Hg 2+ ions from purple red to brown in the presence of ascorbic acid. These colorimetric changes owe the formation of an amalgam of AuNPs enabling the naked eye identification of Hg 2+ ions.

head.ce:title: Smart nano-architectures as potential sensing tools for detecting heavy metal ions in aqueous matrices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The discharge of heavy metal ions into water resources as a result of human activities has become a global issue. Contamination with heavy metal ions poses a major threat to the environment and human health. Therefore, there is a dire need to probe the presence of heavy metal ions in a more selective, facile, quick, cost-effective and sensitive way. Conventional sensors are being utilized to sense heavy metal ions; however, various challenges and limitations like interference, overlapping of oxidation potential, selectivity and sensitivity are associated with them that limit their in-field applicability. Hence, nanomaterial based chemical sensors have emerged as an alternative substitute and are extensively employed for the detection of heavy metal ions as a potent analytical tool. The incorporation of nanomaterials in sensors increases their sensitivity, selectivity, portability, on-site detection capability and device performance. Nanomaterial based electrodes exhibit enhanced performance because surface of electrode at nano-scale level offers high catalytic potential, large active surface area and high conductivity. Therefore, this review addresses the recent progress on chemical sensors based on different nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), metal nanoparticles, graphene, carbon quantum dots and nanocomposites for sensing heavy metals ions using different sensing approaches. Furthermore, various types of optical sensors such as fluorescence, luminescence and colorimetry sensors have been presented in detail.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial, agriculture and domestic sectors are primary sources to discharge heavy metal ions in the eco-system. Some of the heavy metal ions like Zn2+, Mn2+, Fe2+ and Cu2+ are required by living organisms in trace amounts to carry the fundamental processes like metabolism, growth and development of different organs. While some of these heavy metal ions including Cd2+, As3+, Hg2+ and Pb2+ are considered toxic substances and contaminants even at very low concentrations that can be termed as a serious risk to the environment and human health [1–6]. Additionally, there are some natural sources such as erosion of soil and rock, rainwater and weathering from where heavy metals are also added to the environment [7]. On the other hand, leaching of metals from different sources like waste dumps, runoffs, livestock manures and roadwork are some of the other sources (secondary sources) of heavy metal pollution. Fig. 1 illustrates the primary and secondary sources of heavy metal pollution in the eco-system. Heavy metals occur in the form of ions, elements or complexes and are non-degradable.

Furthermore, heavy metals are elements with an atomic number of more than 20 and having a density of higher than 5 g/cm3. They originally belong to metalloids, transition elements and post-transition elements [8,9]. Their accumulation (even at a minute level) in the body causes severe health risks and most of them are carcinogenic [4,10–17]. Maximum concentration limits and linked health risks related to heavy metals have already been reported in the literature [7]. The non-biodegradability, dissolution in water, ability to form aggregates in various parts of the body and delayed half-life makes these species more lethal to human life [18]. The mechanism of their toxicity involves their interaction with the cell proteins and DNA. This interaction leads to the change in protein conformation that in turn results in carcinogenesis. The other detrimental effects of heavy metal ions pollution on human life are lung damage, vomiting, nausea, high blood pressure, fatigue, hepatotoxicity, nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, headache and depression are the main causes of heavy metal ions toxicity ( Fig. 2) [19].

Therefore, the accurate detection and determination of these toxicants is a pressing issue in recent years [20]. Some of the traditionally employed analytical techniques for the detection and determination of these metal ions include microprobes, X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy, capillary electrophoresis, ion chromatography, ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy, atomic emission spectroscopy and atomic absorption spectroscopy [21–23]. These analytical techniques are selective and very sensitive however because of some limitations associated such as (1) High operating expenditures, (2) limits of hiring skilled individuals, (3) expensive and time intensive, (4) complex operating procedures (5) difficult sample preparation and (6) hard for real-time evaluation, limit their in-field applications [20].

This necessitates the development of a new technique that can detect these toxic metal ions with greater simplicity and accuracy. In this regard, chemical sensing has emerged as a promising modality for the accurate and on-site identification of these metal ions. Among a variety of chemical sensors, nanomaterial-based sensors are promising candidates for the purpose. The emergence of nanomaterial-based sensors owes to their distinctive features such as great adsorption power, higher surface reactivity, higher catalytic efficiency and larger surface area [24–28].

A nanosensor consists of three components: (1) nanomaterial, (2) recognition element and (3) signal transducer. Several nanomaterials such as silicon materials, carbon-based materials and metallic nanoparticles (NPs) have been utilized widely to design a nanosensor. Fig. 3 portrays the design and working mechanism of various nanosensors. Nanosensors can be designed to sense a single analyte known as single plex or multiple analytes called multiplex. The higher degree of functionalization, the higher surface to volume ratio and higher reactivity enhance the nanosensor's sensitivity [29]. Furthermore, the formation of nanocomposites, organic-ligand attachment and covalent functionalization play a vital role to improve the selectivity and sensitivity in the identification of heavy metal ions. The reproducibility and lower limit of detection (LOD) are the other important parameters possessed by the nanomaterial-based sensors [22].

This review emphasizes the modern advancement in the area of optical chemical sensors based on different nanomaterials. Furthermore, the designs, working and sensing mechanisms of nanosensors for the detection of heavy metal ions have been discussed in detail. Finally, the conclusion and future recommendations have been presented. Fig. 4 represents different types of nanosensors that are discussed in the present study.

Optical chemical sensors (OCSs) are widely employed for on-site identification of heavy metal ions. The transduction element of OCSs absorbs electromagnetic radiations for producing signals. The specific optical factors get changed by the interaction of radiation with the test sample that could be related to analyte quantity in the sample. Optical chemical sensors worked on the principle of variation of optical characteristics (lifetime, emission, transmission and absorption) because of the linkage of organic dye (immobilized indicator) with analyte [30,31]. Target-induced-aggregation/anti-aggregation and surface alterations of nanomaterials may change the optical characteristics of nanomaterials chemical sensors based on the nanomaterial. In addition, the framework is dependent upon the suitable choice of solid matrix, with appropriate morphology, functionalization and immobilization strategies for these constituents [32]. Fluorescent, surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensors, surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) optical sensors, fluorescence and luminescence sensors have also been demonstrated in this review. Special focus is given to utilization of nanomaterials in the development of these sensors.

Fluorescence based nanosensors consist of the fluorescent element that signals receptor component and binding site to bind the particular analyte [11–14,33–35]. Quantum dots (QDs) have been extensively employed as an alternative to the dyes-based fluorophore. The unique optical characteristics such as high stability towards photo bleaching, narrow emission, great quantum yield and wide range of absorption spectra as well as excellent H2O solubility, great photo-stability and changeable quantum size in a broad emission range make QDs promising alternatives for sensing and biosensing of a variety of analytes [36,37]. Because of broad excitation profiles, multiplexed detection potential, higher quantum efficiencies, size dependent fluorescence emission peaks, great photostability and narrow emission spectra the quantum dots are advantageous to employ as fluorophores [38]. The structures of graphene QDs (GQDs) and carbon dots (CDs) are free from heavy metals.

Moreover, these contain extraordinary surface functionality and very good biocompatibility that makes them very effective candidates for the fluorescent identification of metals [39,40]. Selective linkage sites are formed on the sensor surface that is permitted by molecular imprinting that assists particular linkage of analytes. The facile synthesis and stability are major advantages of formed sensing materials. Based upon the ion-imprinted dual emission quantum dots nanohybrids (CdTe@SiO2@CdSe) the ratiometric-fluorescent probe has been developed for the identification of Cd2+ ions in aqueous samples [41]. Probe structure was found based on covalently bonding green emitting CdSe QDs at the surface of silica nanoparticles that was surrounded by red emitting CdTe QDs. By chemical etching with ethylene-diamine tetra-acetic acid, the particular Cd2+ ions detection sites upon the surface were formed that extinguished the fluorescence (green) of CdSe quantum dots on nanosensor’s external side. By exposing nanohybrid to various quantities of Cd2+ ions, the green fluorescence is progressively reinstated and internal red fluorescence remains the same. As a result, the identifiable fluorescence of different colors was perceived when it is in contact with Cd2+ ions. The ratiometric sensor showed very good sensitivity at optimized conditions with LOD of 25 nM. Another study, reports the development of unique luminescence solid state material (QD-LDH) for on-site concurrent identification of Hg2+, Cd3+ and Pb2+ ions in H2O [42]. The architecture of the nanosensor is based on layered double hydroxides (LDH) and manganese-doped ZnS QDs capped with the glutathione (GSH-Mn-ZnS QDs). The LDH function inhibits the accumulation of quantum dots (QDs) in solid nanocomposite and improved luminescence in aqueous solution as compared to the dispersed quantum dots. Moreover, the chemical and thermal stability of quantum dots is also strengthened by LDH containing nanocomposite. A turn-off response was observed by the introduction of Hg2+, Cd3+ and Pb2+ ions. The luminescence intensity of nanosensor disappeared as the nanosensors come in contact with analytes. Remarkable sensitivity and selectivity were portrayed by the sensor with LOD of 0.93 μM for mixed metal ions. Likewise, the identification of Hg2+ ions in raw-water and tape water samples was carried out with the help of nitrogen-doped-carbon based nanosensor QDs (N-CQDs) that was hydrothermally synthesized as a fluorescent probe [43]. The formation of sensor utilized folic acid (carbon and nitrogen source). For label-free identification of Hg2+ ions the prepared N-CQDs (size approximately 4.5 nm) assisted as a sensitive sensor. Noteworthy quenching of fluorescence intensity is driven by the introduction of Hg2+ ions that lead to non-radiative electron shift from the high energy states to Hg2+ ions d-orbital. The N-CQDs have shown specificity for Hg2+ ions within the existence of a great quantity of other various competitive metal ions and can be termed as “turn-off” fluorescent sensors. The LOD was calculated as low as 0.23 mM. In another documented report, the Gold nano-clusters based CdTe quantum dots were developed. The prepared nanocomposite material showed high selectivity and sensitivity towards fluoride and Hg2+ ions. This nanosensor was developed from a bovine-serum-albumin protein-conjugated Au nanocluster and from tripeptide-capped CdTe quantum dots [44]. The salt-initiated conglomeration upon introduction of Hg2+ ions driven to critical reduction approximately up to 74 % of nanosensor photoluminescence. The LOD towards Hg2+ and fluoride ions was determined as 9 and 117 nM respectively.

Graphene oxide (GO) is another useful nanomaterial that is non-toxic and contains a carboxylic acid group that could be utilized to conjugate the various biomolecules and deoxy-ribonucleic acid functionalized with amine by a covalent bond. Further, GO is an economical fluorescence radiation quencher (acceptor) that may be easily formed on large scale. The fluorescent features of GO can easily be tuned by modifying the surface functionalities. Therefore, graphene oxide obtained great consideration in sensing heavy metals [45]. Ju and his colleagues [39] revealed a simple technique to synthesize the greatly fluorescent nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) for the identification of Fe3+ ions. GQDs are treated hydrothermally with hydrazine for the preparation of N-GQDs as a sensing platform. The presence of oxygen based functional motifs in N-GQDs showed improved fluorescence potential with the blue-shifted-energy that may be due to the existence of nitrogen (electron withdrawing nature) in the structure of nanosensor. The PL intensity of N-GQDs has been reduced by the incorporation of Fe3+ ions. This reduction in PL owes to the chelation of Fe3+ ions with nitrogen (N) of N-GQDs. In real water samples, the LOD of 90 nM was determined by the job’s plot method. Through the electrolysis of graphite, the sulfur-doped-GQDs (S-GQDs) synthesis has been reported [40]. The S-GQDs showed a more sensitive response to Fe3+ ions in comparison to B-GQDs, GQDs and NGQDs. Noteworthy, reduced fluorescence intensity for SGQDs was noticed at 0.7 mM for Fe3+ ions concentration on the other hand the fluorescence of B-GQDs, GQDs and NGQDs remains unaltered. Greater sensitivity in human serum (with LOD of 4.2 nM) was displayed by a probe. For the identification and recognition of Fe3+ ions atoms in H2O, the greatly selective and sensitive graphitic CQDs (GCQDs) were developed through electrochemical ablation of graphite electrodes [46]. The solubility of the probe in water was improved by oxygen containing functional moieties (-COOH and -OH). Moreover, because of the development of complexes between phenolic -OH moieties of GCQDs and Fe3+ ions the probe showed greater sensitivity (LOD of 2 nM). By exfoliation of graphene oxide into the graphene oxide (GO) sheets through ultra-sonication in aqueous media, the GQDs based developing zero-D-materials synthesized within a range of 3–20 nm. In conjunction with the best surface grafting feature, the material also contained carrier transport portability, great surface range and predominant chemical and thermal steadiness. Ran and his co-workers [47] utilized GQDs and silver-based nanoparticles embodied GQDs (AgNPs/ GQDs) for recognition of Ag+ ions and bio-thiol molecules as glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (Hcy). The FL intensity of novel fluorescent material GQDs was decreased after incorporation of Ag+ ions. The reduced fluorescence that was dosage-dependent owes to the synthesis of AgNPs/GQDs hybrids. In addition, when bio-thiols were introduced into the solution, the fluorescence of GQDs was diminished and it has been accredited to Ag-S bond formation. The probe demonstrated ultra-sensitivity with LOD of 3.5 and 6.2 nM for Ag+ ions and Cys respectively. Lu and coworkers [48] exhibited single-step sulfur and oxygen co-doped graphitic carbon nitride QDs (OS-GCNQDs) synthesis through a reaction between thiourea and citric acid in warming conditions. The synthesized OS-GCNQDs showed great quantum yield (14.5 %) and demonstrated variable fluorescence responses in the existence of various metal ions in similar conditions. The PL intensity of OS-GCNQDs has been quenched through the incorporation of the Hg2+ ions that could be because of great affinity of thiourea moieties and amino functional moieties existing on the surface of nanocomposite with Hg2+ ions. The OS-GCNQDs ascertained greatly sensitive that was capable of selectively towards Hg2+ ions with LOD of 0.37 nM. Similarly, other documented reports also investigated the detection of heavy metal ions in aqueous media through graphene and graphene oxide-based optical sensors [48,49]. The addition of graphene oxide or graphene in the nanosensors potentially enhanced the sensitivity in heavy metal detection. For sensing heavy metal ions, metal-based nanomaterials have been widely employed because of their great biocompatibility, easy fabrication, plasmonic features and optical characteristics [28,50,51].

To enhance the detection of heavy metals, metallic nano-structures consisting of aluminum, silver and gold plasmonic NPs have been broadly utilized [52]. For spectral sensing of heavy metals, one-step preparation of CuS, NiS & ZnSNPs, CuSNPs and NiS NPs has been reported [53]. By utilizing tri-sodium citrate and sodium sulfide, the prepared nanoparticles were stabilized in aqueous media. For spectral detection of Pb2+, Cd2+ and Hg2+ ions at very low concentrations, the solid nanoparticles were being utilized as optical sensors. In the existence of ions of heavy metal, absorption potentials of nanoparticles have enhanced and this was connected to basic accumulation of NPs or variations within the nearby atmosphere of the nano substances that produced variations within characteristic surface-plasmon-resonance. For Hg2+ ions identification, NiS NPs were found beneficial while CuS NPs and ZnS NPs were found capable to identify Hg2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions with LOD of 0.1–1 mM and 0.1–1.5 mM, respectively. Li and his colleagues [54] demonstrated the fluorescent nanoprobe based upon L-cysteine (L-Cys) capped Fe3O4@ZnO (Fe3O4@ZnO@ L-Cys) for profoundly specific Fe3+ ions identification. Upon the addition of Fe3+ ions solution, nanosensor fluorescent potential was quenched. This was connected to the interaction of Fe3+ ions with L-Cys. With LOD of 3 nM/L, over a series of competing ions such as Co2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Al3+ and Pb2+ ions the nanoprobe showed high selectivity in identifying Fe3+ ions in serum and wastewater ( Fig. 5).

Shahat and his coworkers [55] synthesized MSNPs based functional material, wherein organic ligand, N,N′ di (3-carboxysalicylidene)-3,4-diamino-5-hydroxypyrazole (DSDH) was attached to the mesoporous silica (MS). Through Vander-Waals forces, covalent bonds and hydrogen bonding the plenteous hydroxyl moieties on MS surface allowed conjugation of DSDH. The obtained material was employed for Co2+ ions identification in aqueous media. The absorbance intensity of the probe was enhanced by introduction of Co2+. This was credited to the interaction between Co2+ ions with DSDH. With LOD of 0.24 mg/L, the probe demonstrated to be sensitive in recognizing Co2+ ions. The synthesis of conjugate nanomaterials (CNMs) has been accomplished with the ligand (organic), ammonium (4-chloro-2- mercaptophenyl) carbamodithioate (ACMPC) that consist of sulfur donor element by utilizing a coordinate reaction approach by functionalizing MSNPs. In another study, Awual and coworkers [56] documented a ligand conjugated nanomaterial for Hg2+ ions identification in an aqueous solution, the resultant sensor was found selective and sensitive toward analyte. As within the case of DSDH, through reversible covalent bonds, Vander Waals force and hydrogen bonding the attachment between SNPs and ACMPC occurs. The color variation from colorless to yellow led to enhance in intensity of absorbance on the addition of Hg2+ ions to CNM solution at optimum pH 5.2 and this was observed through ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy. The formation of (ACMPC)-Hg2+ complex was credited to the selectivity of CNMs. With LOD of 0.26 mg/L, the nanosensor showed high sensitivity in identifying Hg2+ ions. Cao and his coworkers [57] have prepared mono-layer graphite type C3N4 (g-C3N4) as a fluorescence sensor for Ag+ ions identification in real H2O. The sensor reached a LOD of 52.3 nmol/L in optimized conditions for the Ag+ ions without obstructions to the co-existing of miscellaneous metal ions such as Cd2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, K+, Be2+, Fe3+ and Ba2+. Additionally, the obstructions of co-existing Cu2+ ions within the sample could be screened by EDTA chelate incorporation. Preparation of nano-aggregates of pyrene polymer as a probe (fluorescent) for identification of Sr2+ ions in solution has been reported [58]. With LOD of 9 nM, the fluorescent intensity of the probe quenched upon the addition of Sr2+ ions. Over a broad range of competing metals ions such as Pb2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Co3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cs+, K+, Li+ and Na+, the nanosensor displayed high selectivity for detection of Sr2+ ions. The hydrophobic nature of pyrene moieties permitted them at first step stacked in water that driven to excimer emission. Attachment of the Sr2+ ions to host gave alterations in conformation that formed the cavity to fit Sr2+ ions by enhancing gap between pyrene (two) motifs. Consequently, the nanosensor fluorescence intensity was quenched.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LMOFs implication for detecting Persistent metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, metal ions can be classified into three major categories. The group that is detected in a broader sense using LMOFs essentially Al3+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Cr3+ ions, the other group of metal ions comprise Eu3+, Tb3+, Sm3+, Dy3+ ions called repetitive metal ions and lanthanide Ln3+ metal ions respectively, whereas the third group of metal ions is hardly detected using LMOFs. Moreover, the detection limit, applicable strategies, sensitivity, selectivity, structure signal, mechanism response relationships and parameters like Stern -Volmer constant for each metal ion can conduct an efficient sensing approach with adequate detection considerations.

Facile and faster detection of Hg2+ ions for human safety from a catastrophe such as Minamata, whereas LOD is 2 µg/L reported by US Environmental Protection Agency. There are varied methodologies implemented for Hg2+ ions sensing utilizing LMOFs, for example, 1D constituting MOF-composites [59], ratiometrically 2-dimensional detectors [60], chemo-dosimeter detector comprised of MOF [61], approach for probing 2D-dual solvents [62], (d)-LMOFs [63], 2-dimensional probes [64], lanthanide(+3) ions applied for post-improvement of (d)-LMOFs [65], implications of coordination-polymer-nanoparticles (CPNPs) [66]. There are many fruitful merits and incompetent demerits that have been noticed in every category. After the post-synthetic incorporation of Eu3+ ions, Eu3+/CDs@MOF-253 composite represents simultaneously both the CDs centered emission (ICD) and characteristic sharp emissions of Eu3+ ions originating from the Eu3+ ions lowest emitting state. Further, IEu3+ showed no considerable variation while 1-dimensional quenching effect was revealed in IQDs by way of enclosed quantum dots (QDs) surface-association of oxygen atoms and Hg2+ ions. Consequently, an increment of 7.5 multiple was indicated by IEu3+/IQDs, in comparison to the primary ratio. Proficient discriminativeness of Eu3+/QDs@MOF-253 having 13 nM LOD for Hg2+ ions affirmed such effect merely in Hg2+ ions, although different metal ions were also available. In addition, Hg2+ ions could cause red coloration altered from blue suggesting the above-mentioned composite to perform like colorimetric probe [60]. Remarkable chemical alteration including covalent bond manufacturing and splitting provides the basis for non-reversible conversion of obvious signal accompanying identification of analyte using chemodosimeter sensors.

Owing to the existence of bulky butyne groups, rotation of carbonyl group was hindered resulting in FL responsive UiO-66@Butyne. Through an irreversible oxymercuration reaction, UiO-66 @Butyne exhibits a 1D quenching response to Hg2+ ions. The loss of alkyne functionality was shown by a decreased peak at 2950 cm−1 in FT-IR spectroscopy after treatment with Hg2+ ions. The presence of concentrated sulfide is unfavorable because compounds comprising sulfur and nitrogen atoms experience oxidative cleavages at ambient conditions when preserved for a longer duration, although Hg2+ ions have been appropriately diagnosed via LMOFs inhabiting these moieties. These display non-renewable sensitivity in comparison to other probes like fluoro-/chemo-sensors. Fabrication of linking agent-functionalized (d)-LMOFs has been considered a high potential category for sensing Hg2+ ions. Nitrogen donor-guest communication parts are a vital constituent of functional ligands [67], sulfur-supported were on the other hand [63] also few residing oxygen units [67]. Scarcely, variation in molecular design or molecular breakage could affect the procedure of identifying Hg2+ ions [63]. Detection-limit of 9.9 pM demonstrated higher sensitivity for Hg2+ ions by a zinc-based MOF with the representation of [{Zn(4,4′-AP)(5-AIA)}.(DMF)]0.5n , and proved with photoluminescent measurements. The introduction of Hg2+ ions brought a change in the stretching peak position of carbon and nitrogen bond as shown in FT-IR measurements. Moreover, nitrogen (1 s) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy measurements for [{Zn(4,4′-AP)(5-AIA)}.(DMF)]0.5n shows a peak at ∼403.57 eV for coordinated-amino group, ∼402.36 eV for azo-group and ∼399.10 eV for pyridine-group, enclosed in a wide peak at ∼400 eV. Interaction of Hg2+ ions with [{Zn(4,4′-AP)(5-AIA)}.(DMF)]0.5n residing nitrogen units resulted in shifting of peaks in the redder zone around ∼405.53 eV, attributable to Hg2+ ions incorporation. The Hg2+ ions receive electrons from azo-groups during communication of N2 group with Hg2+ ions as displayed by the practical investigations. Until now, literature explained higher KSV and LOD for responsive and discriminative identification of Hg2+ ions adopting particular soft N-constituting impressive architecture of d-LMOFs. Formerly, a small FL shoulder is exhibited at around 410 nm while a bright Photo-luminescent radiance has been noticed at around 350 nm for Cd-EDDA skeleton. The above given explanation corresponds to LLCT and LMCT reactions (processes), accordingly [64]. The use of Cd-EDDA in metal ions sensing reveals that as the Hg2+ ions concentration rises that is connected to the LMCT process. The obtained Stern-Volmer constant (KSV) 4.3 mM and identification-limit 10 nM were observed in the presence of 0.21 mM Hg2+ ions and 8.2 times improvement with the given ratio. Ligand’s reformation and the existence of Hg2+ ions were considered the fundamental requirements for Cd-EDDA compound breakage which consequently reduced LMCT and were confirmed with mass-spectroscopy, FT-IR and PXRD. Thus, in the event of rapid simultaneous presence of LLCT and LMCT processes in PL emission of (d)-LMOF, the ratio of ILLCT/ILMCT may be considered as a 2D ratiometric response in the sense that when the framework collapses, the ratio of ILLCT/ILMCT may be considered as a 2D ratiometric response. The ILMCT will drop, while the ILLCT could increase due to ligand–metal ion complexation resulting in an increase in 2D ratiometric ILLCT/ILMCT response. The detection limit of Hg2+ ion sensing employing LMOFs has been reported to be in the order of magnitude equal to nM level [64,68]. Simultaneously, variation in the compound's photo luminescent radiance intensity occurred owing to shifting of electronic cloud from compound to Hg2+ ions resulted due to communication of Hg2+ ions and functional-ligand, particularly nitrogen donors, residing zinc-LMOF demonstrated ligand rooted radiance while concentrating the connection between mechanism response and structure signal. Although, efficient fabrication of potential Zn2+-LMOFs due to its dissolution in H2O and photo-durability of the structure are demanded while reproducibility of Zn2+-LMOFs depend on specified functional ligands. The LOD up to level of pM were noticed for gold nanocomposites@UiO-66 [69] and DSM@7 [70], built on MOF. In addition, KSV value for Eu3+@post-improved UiO-66 approached to μM level. Appropriate incorporation of enclosed guest compounds inside porous MOF, employs MOF-supported composite to recognize Hg2+ ions proficiently nonetheless, percolation of such kind of sensors must be thoroughly analyzed for practical implications.

Al3+ ions in high amount not only affects the well-being of humans but also can cause serious damage to the human body since it is not a scarce constituent essential for humans. This is a reduction in the haste with which Al3+ ions are detected. Many efforts have been made to detect Al3+ ions using LMOFs and several methodologies have been used in this line. The sensors extinguishing framework of emissive LMOFs resulting in host-signal alteration in response generation and signal transduction are termed as sacrificial sensors. The bonding of organic-ligand and Al3+ ions supports various redundant reaction procedures. Intending to recognize Al3+ ions, Singha et al. [71] applied 3,5-diamino-1,2,4-triazole (DATZ) and 4,4′-oxybis(benzoic acid) (H2OBA) to synthesize [Co(OBA)(DATZ)0.5(H2O)], a triazole-supported (d)-LMOF. Aromatic ligands attached with metal ions underwent intra-ligand π *-n and π *- π * electronic transitions instigating λmax around 407 nm while the excitation wavelength was 283 nm. In comparison to preliminary emission, translocation of λmax approximately 24 nm with a signal intensification multiple of 6.3 was noticed when [Co (OBA) (DATZ)0.5(H2O)] ((d)-LMOF) was implemented for ion identification, however, displayed insignificant variation in the presence of contrasting metal ions. The attachment of free N-atoms constituent of triazole-ligand (-NH-) and Al3+ ions supplied a source for signal-transduction discriminatively. Other metal ions interact with free amine groups via Lewis acid-base interactions, whereas Al3+ ions can subtract an H+ from the NH group of the triazole ring and create triazolate with the amine groups (NH2) acting as Lewis basic sites due to their high charge-to-size ratio. The blue shift in PL emission peak of frameworks is caused by electrostatic interaction between Al3+ ions and the triazolate ring. Furthermore, triazolate production enhances the overall electron density on DATZ, resulting in turn-on luminescence behavior. Because of the limited possibility of forming a triazolate ring in acidic conditions, the results reveal a 2.8 times enhancement that is approximately 3 times less than the value obtained in the initial pure aqueous medium. Based on the strength of architectural uniqueness, revocable pathway and discriminative identification of Al3+ ions, it was recommended as an excellent instance of chemical communication betwixt compound and metal ions. Additionally, the maximum LOD was found to be remarkably at high-level in comparison with [Co (OBA) (DATZ)0.5(H2O)] that was observed with a detection limit of 57.6 μg/L. Al3+ ions showed LOD of 7.6 mM and 200 μg/L for drinkable H2O, as suggested by Environmental Protection Agency. Thus, considering it an excellent instance for the appropriate detection limit of renewable and discriminative sensors by slowing down. With intention of preparing [{Cd2(syn-dftpmcp) (1,3-BDC)2}0.5DMF·H2O]n, Li et al. [72] applied post synthesis photo-dimerization process, while (syn-dftpmcp stands for syn-3,4,12,13-tetrakis(4-pyridyl)− 8,17-bisfluoro-1,2,9,10-diethano[2.2] metacyclophane). The results of metal ions detection in CH3CN demonstrate that in the presence of 1 mM/L Al3+ ions solution, PL emission of framework is fully quenched with an LOD of 183 μg/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: More s-block and d-block metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The identification of metal ions such as Zn2+, Ru2+, Tl2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, In3+, Ga3+, Fe2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Bi3+, Ba2+ and Ag+ using LMOFs in comparison to previously reported Fe3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Al3+ and Hg2+ ions are limited. In addition, such metal ions could be detected with strategies like mechanism response and structure signal, used to implied for redundant metal ions [15–17, 73, 74]. With intention of favorable probing of Pd2+ ions, although, the metal ions are redundantly detected following linker metal ions bonding. As a result, greater research into the detection of these metal ions by LMOFs is required due to the environmental threats and harms that these metal ions cause to humans, as well as the development of information about LMOFs in metal ions detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions from lanthanide series
ce:section.ce:para: Unlike the non-radiative d-d transitions in (d)-LMOFs, the f-f transitions in Ln3+ ions are radiative if Ln3+ ions centers are effectively sensitized through the antenna effect of the organic ligand and the LMREnT process occurs. Depending upon the unique emission maxima of Ln3+ ions, enhanced photoluminescence results in proficient LMREnT. Succeeding post-synthesizing Ln3+@MOF-composites, the fundamental factor leading to sensitize Ln3+ ions, probing metal ions and emissive character of (f)-LMOFs was supposed to be REnT. Furthermore, the metal ions linking, exchanging cation, configuration breakdown and overlapping manners that are responsible for 1-dimensional improvement or diminishing of λ max of transduced signal, adopted by (f)-LMOFs employed to sense metal ions. On account of proficient MOF-to- Ln3+-REnT, 1-dimensional turn on the character of specific max of Ln3+ ions would be responsible for signal generation after application MOFs to sense Ln3+ ions. Because of this reason, Sm3+, Dy3+, Tb3+ and Eu3+ ions were scrutinized out of Ln3+ ions series. With the application of UV light, colorimetric recognition and 1-dimensional turn-on stimulation could be observed concurrently. As mentioned above, the color variation in MOF to Ln3+ ions could be observed bare eye under UV radiation source unveiling their sensitivity, e.g., Sm3+ ions showed orange, Dy3+ ions displayed blue, Tb3+ ions were detected with green and Eu3+ ions were observed with red color. The appearance shade of sensing agent alters after interaction of MOFs and Ln3+ ions such as Eu3+ ions show red color [75], Tb3+ ions give green [76], Dy3+ ions exhibit blue [77] and Sm3+ ions unveil orange color [77]. On the other hand, fabrication of Ln3+@MOF-composites has been widely administered for the identification of varied samples following an efficient approach of sensitizing Ln3+ ions. Free functional-ligands co-ordinational manner, inter-change of anions from a porous framework with cations and pores impregnation with Ln3+ ions are the most practiced procedures for preparation of Ln3+@MOF-composites for sensitizing Ln3+ ions and application of MOFs for Ln3+ ions identification. Comprehensively, high proficiency has been acquired with furnishing suitable factors for sensing and identification of Ln3+ ions [77].

The fluorescent nanosensors have been placed in fascinating class of molecular devices for the detection of various heavy metals in the environment. Various organic dyes such as rhodamine, fluorescein and cyanine are being utilized as fluorescent probes in the sensors. Although, a limited excitation spectrum, little signals, low absorption coefficients, less stability and photo bleaching are associated disadvantages [78]. During the excitation process, the dyes exhibit robust phototoxic impacts to create ROS. The basic simple principle of fluorescence sensors is constructed upon particular interaction between the target analyte (metal ion) and detection component (nanomaterial) to initiate alterations in the fluorescence features of nanomaterial [79]. Recently by employing the different nanomaterials the fluorescence sensors have been developed with enhanced selectivity and sensitivity along with nanocomposite synthesis and functionalization with linkers which may be organic. Nanomaterials improved the identification range, on-site identification potential, stability and also efficient performance of fluorescent based sensors [80]. The biomolecules existing on nanomaterial surface help binding of the analyte. The NM-metal ions interactions are commonly concerned with direct fluorescent quenching or energy transference phenomena (FRET, PET, NSET) that lead to the detection of analyte [4,11,81]. Many fluorescent nanomaterials such as MOFs, polymer dots, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), semiconductor quantum dabs (QDs), silver nanoclusters (AgNCs), gold (AuNCs), up-conversion NPs (UCNPs), and metallic NPs explored for metal ions sensors development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal NPs and CNTs based sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The employment of various nanoparticles (UCNPs, metallic nanoparticles etc.) in fluorescent sensors for the identification of heavy metal ions has attained more attraction because of high bio-compatibility, great specificity, facile synthesis, functionalization of surface and optical properties [50,82]. Features associated with metallic nanoparticles made them ideal for the development of fluorescent probes with a potential optical array [83]. For the identification of Pb2+ ions, many fluorescent nanoparticles have attained attention. For example, in the existence of Pb2+ ions, the CuNPs templated with poly-thymine (poly-T) displayed quenching [84]. With great selectivity against other various metal particles in ideal conditions, the nanosensor exhibited a LOD of 0.4 nM along with a fast identification time (∼10 min). This process proved authenticated with tap H2O along with a recovery of 99.1 %.

Similarly, label free procedure established by employing the DNA template CuNPs as fluorescent nano-probes for the identification of Pb2+ ions [85]. During the NPs synthesis, it was demonstrated that CuNPs were stabilized by dsDNA encased by Cu2+ ions. Metallophilic reactions resulted between Cu2+ and Pb2+ by the addition of Pb2+ ions existing at CuNP surfaces, in this manner inducing fluorescence quenching. With the enhancing Pb2+ ions concentration within a range of 5–100 nM (LOD = 5 nM), the fluorescence intensity of NPs reduced. Further, biosynthesized silver nanoparticles were used as fluorescent nano-probes for the advancement of turn-on type Pb2+ ions sensors in water [86]. Moreover, functionalized silver nanoparticles with the organic compounds served as an excellent sensing site for metal ions identification. For instance, silver nanoparticles that are modified with catechin are utilized for the formation of a more specific fluorescent Pb2+ ions sensor [87]. The presence of hydroxyl moieties in catechin initiate the reduction of gold chloride (AuCl4 -) ions to produce gold-based nanoparticles. However, on the introduction of Pb2+ ions to the matrix, the complexes of Pb2+-Au and Pb2+-catechin were being developed on the gold nanoparticles surface because of electron rich hydroxyl moieties and this enhanced the catalytic potential of gold-based nanoparticles similar to peroxidases. For instance, in the existence of H2O2 the oxidized amplex ultra red (AUR) probe exhibited the robust intensity of the fluorescence. A linear relation between AUR intensity and Pb2+ ions concentration with LOD of 1.5 nM was exhibited by H2O2-Catechin-AUR-AuNP probe. Gold based nanoparticles (AuNPs) were being utilized in association with Brilliant Cresyl Blue (BCB) dye for the formation of a FRET-based fluorescent nano-sensor in the near infrared range [88]. The restoration of BCB dye fluorescence occurs because of dye molecules detachment from the gold-based nanoparticles that occurs due to the addition of the Pb2+ ions. At optimum conditions, the chelation reaction happened between glutathione capped gold nanoparticles and Pb2+ ions. The Pb2+ ions concentration could be identified by the nanosensor in the range of 0.16–2.1 ng/mL. The nanosensor showed great sensitivity and insignificant interference because of absorbance in NIR region. Gold nanoparticles have been employed as significant fluorescence quencher in NSET method because they have capability to multiply the transfer of energy process [89,90].

For the optical identification of metallic ions (Pb2+ and Hg2+), the gold nanoparticles are used in the conjugation with aptamers. The linear relationship between the concentration of these ions and fluorescence intensity was exhibited in the range of 10 pM to 1 μM. Furthermore, the LOD of 27 pM for Pb2+ and 51 pM for Hg2+ was calculated by using the equation 3σ/slope in 10 mM sodium phosphate buffer. On the other hand, the LOD for Pb2+ and Hg2+ in human blood serum was as low as 128 pM and 121 pM respectively [91]. To enhance the selectivity and sensitivity of the fluorescent nanosensors, the employment of up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) doped with the metal/lanthanide ions attracts a great amount of attention during the last decades. Up-conversion nanoparticles have displayed excellent photo-luminescent characteristics through the following mechanism of dual/multi-photon. Further, these have capability to transform longer wavelengths to shorter wavelength fluorescent emissions [92]. In FRET-based Pb2+ ions nanosensor, the up-conversion nanoparticles of NaYF4 functionalized with the ethylene-imine polymer and gold nanoparticles capped with the 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (MUA) utilized as acceptors and donors respectively [93]. The mechanism of identification is based upon ions chelation that detached Up-conversion NPs from gold nanoparticles in the existence of the Pb2+ ions which results in donor fluorescence restoration. With the LOD of 20 nM, the linear identification limit for the established sensor was described as between 0.5 and 10 μM.

Based on gold nano rods (AuNRs) and NaYF4: Yb, Ho up-conversion nanoparticles, the dual FRET based apta sensor was being formed for the identification of the metal ions [94]. Firstly, the up-conversion nanoparticles improved with amino (-NH2) moiety associated aptamers through covalently bond and gold nanorods functionalized with thiol-modified corresponding DNA sequences of the Pb2+ ions specific DNAzymes. The up-conversion fluorescence (green and red) of UCNPs was quenched because of instantaneous overlapping between absorption spectra of gold nanoparticles and fluorescence emission spectra of up-conversion nanoparticles. Up-conversion fluorescence was restored by the introduction of the Pb2+ ions to the matrix, because of the development of G-quadruplex structure between the Pb2+ ions and aptamers. Between Pb2+ ions concentration and nanoparticles fluorescence intensity, the enhanced linear relationship was observed within the range of 0.1–100 nM (LOD = 50 pM). By utilizing the human blood serum samples and the environmentally polluted food samples, the nanosensor was examined with a restoration value between 96 % and 110.6 % [94]. Recently for Pb2+ ions identification, the NaYF4/Yb3+/Tm3+ UCNPs were used as FRET donors while TGA capped CdTe QDs employed as FRET acceptors in nanosensors [95]. The advanced system identified Pb2+ ions in human serum with great capability to overcome the auto-fluorescence of serum with LOD of 80 nm. For the identification of Pb2+ ions, the fluorescent probes such as AuNCs, AuNRs and AgNCs utilization are the point of interest these days [96–99]. For example, based on fluorescence quenching the glutathione functionalized gold nanorods have been reported for selective Pb2+ ions sensing [100]. The nanosensor exhibited great sensitivity in an array of 5 nM to 5 mΜ with LOD of 2 nM. In the same way as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for the identification of Pb2+ ions, a hybrid of SiNPs connected with AuNCs/QDs was utilized [101]. Based on ratiometric intensity (I520/I620) with various Pb2+ ions concentrations from 0 to 250 nM (LOD = 3.5 nM) the fluorescence quenching capability of the probe was calculated. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) strips coated with AuNC/QD hybrid structures are employed for the identification of Pb2+ ions in mineral water, seawater, ground water and tap water. A metal ion identification range of 0.1 μM was shown by the solid sensor strip [101]. Similarly, for the identification of Pb2+ ions in living cells the glutathione (GSH) capped fluorescent silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) have been reported [102]. The identification was based on quenching (fluorescence) because of the coordination connections between Pb2+ ions and carboxy/amino moieties existing on GSH-AgNCs (LOD = 0.2 nM). Silver nanoclusters have been templated with the PS2.M-DNA to form a Pb2+ ions nano-biosensor [102]. Fluorescence was enhanced by the introduction of Pb2+ ions into the sensor via G-quadruplex development from aptamer-DNA. The two dark AgNC-DNAs situated at 3′ and 5′ terminals exhibited fluorescence and these were produced due to alteration in DNA-aptamer conformation. With LOD of 3 nM, the higher sensitivity was shown by the nanosensor in the range of 5–50 nM. For selectively identifying the Pb2+ ions, recently the gold nanoclusters capped with the MPA and GSH have been produced by employing core-etching and ligand change reaction [103]. Big clusters of non-luminescent gold nanoclusters have been produced due to aggregation of gold nanoclusters as well as the interaction between Pb2+ ion and thiol moiety present on surface of gold nanoclusters. In natural drinking water, the sensor has LOD of 10 nM. In the identification of metal ions, the CNTs also described as an outstanding fluorescence quencher other than metal nanoparticles [104]. For example, for formation of multi-plexed aptamer metal ions identification system the fluorescence quenching features of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) were being utilized [105] and system exhibited the LOD of 20 nM. For higher sensitive Pb2+ ions sensing, the CNTs functionalized by ATTO-647 N/aptamers have been developed [106]. With LOD of 0.42 nM, the developed aptasensor exhibited significant sensing of Pb2+ ions in tap H2O and in serum samples. Fluorescent nanosensors behaved as amazing sensing tool for practical applications due to designed nanomaterials.

A very attractive class of fluorescent nanomaterials is the carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/carbon dots (CDs) with many amazing characteristics such as the existence of numerous functionalities, cost-effective, facile preparation, tunable emissions, strong photoluminescence, the potential of surface functionalization, bio-compatibility, good photo-stability and very good water solubility [107]. In sensing various kinds of heavy metal ions like Zn2+, Cr6+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Se4+, As3+, Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions the CQDs are being employed extensively [108,109]. Biocompatible and non-toxic CQDs are obtained from natural carbon resources [110]. The general detecting mechanisms of CQD sensors are fluorescence quenching, fluorescence activation, PET, FRET and inside filter effect ( Fig. 6).

For example, in chocolate, H2O soluble fluorescent CQDs are produced by single step hydro-thermal reaction [111]. The prepared CQDs based sensor is employed for the detection of Pb2+ ions. Great sensitivity was exhibited by nanosensor because of fluorescent quenching that was based on an interaction between Pb2+ ions and hydroxyl moieties upon the CQDs. For Pb2+ ions detection, the CQDs prepared from the Osmium Sanctum were also utilized [112]. Fluorescence of CQDs is greatly quenched in the presence of Pb2+ ions because of electron transference (non-radiative) in which amine moiety existing on surfaces of CQD interacted with Pb2+ ions (empty d-orbitals). In the same manner, the preparation of nitrogen-doped CQDs was carried out by employing the Lantana camera berries (as a source of carbon) and for the doping agent, EDTA was used [113]. In human serum and real water samples, the CQDs (QY= 33.15 %) were being utilized as an amazing sensing agent for the identification of Pb2+ ions with the sensing limit from 0 to 200 nM with 9.6 nM LOD. The Gingko biloba leaf extracts were utilized for the derivation of flavonoid doped CQDs these were used for the development of a Pb2+ ions sensor [114]. Flavonoid glycosides present on the surface of CQDs were being specifically bonded by Pb2+ ions and this led to fluorescence quenching. With a LOD of 55 μM, the Pb2+ ions detected by the nanosensor were within the 1–20 nM range. Through dipole-dipole force and hydrogen bonding, the agarose hydrogel was further used for flavonoid-CQDs doping to develop a detecting system. With 12.89 nM LOD the gel slices were utilized for the removal and identification of Pb2+ ions [114]. For fluorescence and colorimetric-based Pb2+ ions sensing in water samples, the fabrication of the CQDs with table sugar was carried out [114]. Turbidimeter was employed to quantify the Pb2+ ions concentration with excellent selectivity (LOD = 14 μg/L) that was close to the permissible limit of Pb2+ ions in drinking H2O (15 μg/L). Similarly, the dextrose ager also known as biological media was employed for the carbon nano-rods preparations through microwave heating for the formation of an effective lead sensor [115]. The multicolor fluorescence was exhibited by the multicolor CQDs and water-soluble CQDs. Pb2+ ions developed a complex by coordinating with hydroxyl and carboxylic moieties existing upon CQDs surface that initiate the fluorescence quenching via PET mechanism (with LOD 0.73 nM and 1.1 nM in tap water samples and river water samples respectively [116]. On the cellulose paper strips the CQDs were additionally embedded that exhibited the identification limit of 106 μM [115,116]. For Pb2+ ions sensing in aqueous solution, the chemically prepared CQDs were also utilized. Nitrogen doped CQDs (N-CQDs) were synthesized using glycerol and EDTA (under microwave radiations exhibiting LOD of 0.1–6 μM [117]. Fluorescent CQDs were synthesized using bovine serum albumin protein through single step method (LOD = 5.05 μM) [118]. Conjugation of N-CQDs was done with magnetic NPs (Fe3O4 nanoparticles) for the identification and adsorption of Pb2+ ions [118]. For the pre-concentration and solid-phase extraction of Pb2+ ions from vegetative samples and H2O samples, N-CQD/ Fe3O4 NP hybrid adsorbent material was utilized. The presence of functional moieties (hydroxyl, carboxylic and C-O-C-) on CQDs permitted great Pb2+ ions adsorption during extraction with an identification range of 0.3–300 μg/L [39]. Moreover, for the development of a turn-off/on Pb2+ ions sensor the CQDs were also utilized. Thus, for the instantaneous identification of pyrophosphate (PPi) and Pb2+ ions, highly fluorescent and water-soluble CQDs were obtained from kerosene soot [119]. The interaction of Pb2+ ions with carboxyl moieties existing on the surface of CQDs lead to reduce the CQDs intensity of fluorescence. By incorporation of PPi in the system, fluorescence was regained because of the great binding capability to Pb2+ ions than CQDs. With LOD of 2 nM, the Pb2+ ions were identified in the range of 0.5–11 μM. By utilizing the sodium-citrate and poly-acrylamide (precursors) the blue fluorescent CQDs were prepared for the advancement of “turn-off/on” Pb2+ ions sensor with a detection limit of 4.6 nM [120]. For the development of “turn-off/on type” Pb2+ ions sensor, boron doped CQDs are reported [121]. The chelation between Pb2+ ions and oxygen atoms existing on the surface of CQD forms complex (non-fluorescent) with DL of 25–250 μM. The N-CQDs acquired from pyrolysis of citric acid and ethanol-amine have been demonstrated for the formation of “turn-on” type Pb2+ ions sensor [122]. Initially, due to interaction (electrostatic) between the carboxyl moieties of PAAs and amino groups of CQDs (range 0–1.67 mM and LOD of 22.8 μM), the fluorescence of immobilized CQDs (at λmax 430 nm) was quenched. For the identification of bio-molecules, analytes and metals, CQD based ratiometric fluorescent method have been employed [70]. Through solvothermal process, the multiemissive CQD hybrids were synthesized from bamboo leaves [70]. Hybrids of CQDs were synthesized in existence of sodium carbonate to acquire triple emissive CQDs at a single wavelength of 400 nm. The Pb2+ ions interacted with flavonoid moieties existing on surface of CQD with quenching fluorescence of CQDs @ 493 nm and improved signal @ 653 nm. The fluorescence response plotted against Pb2+ ions concentration to get detection range from 0.6 to 800 nM (with LOD of 0.14 nM). In another study, CQDs were produced by utilizing the glutathione and formamide under microwave method by employing a ratiometric fluorescent sensing system [123]. The CQDs (QY 6.49 %) exhibited fluorescence and absorption both in blue and red spectral regions at the same time. The interactions between the Pb2+ ions and surface groups (-COOH, CN, CS) of the CQDs are driven to such fluorescence quenching. With a LOD of 37.1 nM, the double fluorescent CQDs exhibited a greater sensitivity within linear range (1–961 nM). For FRET-based identification of Pb2+ ions, another ratiometric fluorescent sensor was created utilizing amino capped CQDs and the GSH-functionalized gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) [124]. The CQDs (positively charged) served as energy donor and gold nanoclusters (negatively charged) employed as energy acceptors and both of these were utilized as FRET pair in research work. When excited at a wavelength of 380 nm the fluorescent probe exhibited double crests at 440 and 565 nm. The introduction of Pb2+ ions enhanced the gold nanoclusters fluorescence while this did not affect the CQDs. This happened because thiolated gold nanoclusters exhibited photo-luminescence on their aggregation [125]. Within the limit of 2–60 μM and with an LOD of 0.5 μM, the created ratiometric sensor showed a direct relationship between Pb2+ ions concentration and probe ratiometric intensity (I565/I440). In view of these improvements and a broad ubiquity of CQDs as a lead detecting material, it could be concluded that environment friendly CQD-based sensing probes will coordinate with simpler readout frameworks and imaging helped quantification strategies in the future.

Nanoparticles such as gold, silver and lead have peroxidase mimetic potential and the gathering of these nanoparticles with graphene can increase their stability, in addition, provide them with excellent catalytic potential. By utilizing this characteristic, several sensors for heavy metal ions have been proposed. It was evident that GO-AuNP nanohybrids with peroxidase type potential would distinguish between the single and double-stranded deoxy-ribonucleic acid (DNA) and then Pb2+, Hg2+ ions could be colorimetrically identified based on the metal ion activated DNA conformation development [126]. According to a research report, the existence of Hg2+ could increase and improve the peroxidase mimetic potential of reduced graphene oxide/poly-ethyleneimine/palladium-nanoparticles (rGO/PEI/Pd) nanohybrids enhancing the oxidation and color variation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetra-methylbenzidine in solution and recognizing selective identification of Hg2+ ions with LOD of 0.39 nM ( Fig. 7) [127].

Zhi and coworkers [128] made graphene-doped MoS2 aerogels to support Au/Fe3O4 nano-particles. Fe3O4 nanoparticles bestowed magnetic features to aerogels. The prepared nanohybrid materials facilitate facile reusability and recovery of catalyst. Further, the detection was supported by the addition of gold nanoparticles in an aqueous medium. The formed Hg2+ ions contained aerogel that stimulated peroxidase mimetic potential. In addition to the per-oxidase mimetic potential, upon introduction of Hg2+ ions the direct solution color change of the nanocomposites has also been referred to as the detection of Hg2+ ions. By following a simple one-pot oxidation-reduction reaction, Yan and his colleagues [129] prepared multi-functional graphene gold nanocomposites (G-AuNPs). The formed G-AuNPs initiate a colorimetric change upon introduction of Hg2+ ions from purple red to brown in the presence of ascorbic acid. These colorimetric changes owe the formation of an amalgam of AuNPs enabling the naked eye identification of Hg2+ ions.

Graphene oxide and graphene have been used (as substrate) for loading of fluorophore through direct assembling of fluorescent nanoparticles or by employing biomolecules to capture the fluorophores. The simultaneous removal and identification of Hg2+ ions was carried out using rGO-Fe3O4 functionalized with Hg2+ ions specific T-DNA [130]. In another study, the detection of Hg2+ ions with double-gold nanoclusters/graphene oxide (D-GNCs/GO) has been reported via electrostatic interactions. where D-GNCs enhance the detection sensitivity and graphene oxide can stimulate the rate of reaction [131]. Graphene oxide decorated with AuNCs@BSA is employed for the identification and isolation of Hg2+ ions based on the metal binding characteristic of AuNCs@BSA nanofibers and large surface area of graphene oxide. It was assumed that the high separation effectiveness of the hybrid membrane can be ascribed to the uncommon Hg2+-Au+ interactions, Hg2+-cysteine residue binding, and the hybrid membrane ( Fig. 8) [132]. Liu et al. [133] adjusted graphene oxide with Ag-In-Zn-S quantum dabs (QDs). The addition of Cu2+ ions would initiate the accumulation of QDs and consequent fluorescence quenching can be utilized for Cu2+ ions assurance.

Dye-labelled ssDNA by hydrophobic and π − π interactions may be well adsorbed by graphene oxide and at the same time quench the fluorescence of dyes resonance by FRET, whereas dsDNA or other DNAs are not able to bind to the surface of graphene oxide. These features made graphene oxide an attractive and efficient DNA based fluorescent sensing system for the identification of heavy metal ions. FRET/shell-insulated metal nanoparticle surface-enhanced fluorescence spectroscopy (FRET/SIMNSEF) protocol having graphene oxide and anisotropic shell-insulated metallic nanoparticles (SIMNs) demonstrated for the sensing of Hg2+ ions by T-rich ssDNA functionalization. While the absence of Hg2+ ions, fluorophore-labelled ssDNA adorned on SIMNs adsorb on the surface of graphene oxide and thus the fluorescence could be quenched by the FRET. DNA hairpin framework formation is due to THg2+-T coordination. Furthermore, the decreased interaction between the graphene oxide and DNA provides ultimate fluorescence restoration and further the surface enhanced fluorescence (SEF) effect increase and amplify the fluorescence signal, realizing the sensitive identification of Hg2+ ions [134]. Two hairpin probes and a helper DNA have been designed and these could be adsorbed on graphene oxide to quench the fluorescence of the label. The hybridization chain reactions (HCRs) between the two hairpin probes started through the Hg2+ ions employing the helper DNA by T- Hg2+-T coordination chemistry. The dsDNA products were released from the graphene oxide and the fluorescence was restored. The methodology can be applied in sensitive Hg2+ ion sensing with a LOD of 0.3 nM [135].

Similarly, Hg2+ (Ag+, Pb2+) ions sensing platforms have been constructed based on fluorescence variation because of conformation DNA development on combining with Hg2+ (Ag+, Pb2+) ions to create the T- Hg2+-T (C-Ag+-C, Pb2+-G-quadruplex) base pairs [136–138]. The sensing platforms for Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions identification have been proposed on basis of quenching nanoparticles fluorescence [139,140]. As a linker to bridge graphene oxide and GQDs another distinctive technique utilized Grich DNA and after that to initiate the formation of G-quadruplex the Pb2+ ions were utilized. Leading to the shortening of the distance between graphene oxide and GQDs leading to an alteration within the chain length, in this way fluorescence quenching was observed in GQDs and this happened due to energy transfer between graphene oxide and GQDs [141]. Metal ion specific DNAzymes have been utilized for the construction of the detecting platform. Liu and his colleagues [142] reported the Cu2+ ions dependent DNAzyme having a 30-FAM-developed Cu-Sub and a Cu-Enzyme can be gathered with graphene, resulting in close proximity of fluorophore to the surface of graphene to assist the complete quenching. The self-gathered graphene DNAzyme configuration disturbed by the proximity of Cu2+ ions would cleave the DNA substrate hence discharging FAM-labeled DNA strand to produce increased Cu2+ ions concentration dependent fluorescence signals. FAM-labeled were also replaced by fluorophore [143]. For the examination of Pb2+ ions, the DNAzyme principle was implemented [144]. To create an inflexible structure with little ssDNA circles they utilized Pb2+ ions dependent 8–17 DNAzymes to hybridize with the Cy3-labeled substrate that might not tie effectively to graphene oxide, and in this way, the fluorescence of Cy3 might not be quenched. Upon Pb2+ ions addition, the Cy3- labelled part was discharged and 17 S substrate strand was cleaved, leading to the strong quenching of the fluorescence flag by graphene oxide. The obtained results were controversial [145]. Different fluorescence responses in reference [145] may be due to various lengths and configurations of DNAzyme-substrates and different affinities of discharged fragments to graphene/graphene oxide. Molecular beacon (MB) having fluorophore and a quencher in near vicinity with quenched fluorescence and furthermore the graphene oxide adsorption can reduce the back-ground fluorescence. The Cu2+ ions and H2O2 existence may break the molecular beacons into small portions that can be released from surface of graphene oxide which restores the Cu2+ ions dependent fluorescence [146].

Surface-layer of rGO was passivated and its FL intensified after improvement with T following SN2 methodology, demonstrated by Dinda and coworkers [147]. Grounding on Thymine- Hg2+ -T communication, Hg2+ ions recognition has been tripped by applying the thermally reduced graphene oxide as a fluorescence detector. On the account of elevated bonding fascination of Hg2+ and I− ions, the I− ions could be identified with greater ease. A remarkable refurbishment and revamping in the fluorescence of graphene was proposed by Fu and coworker [148] that in the existence of S2O3 2- and HOCH2CH2SH, Pb2+ ions increased the percolating speed of gold-NPs that in-turn was produced quenching of graphene fluorescence intensity. Moreover, mineral water and tap water specimens are also supplied with the same sensor for estimating Pb2+ ions. Ag+ and Hg2+ ions sensing has also been acquired with the nearness of these ions transferring electrons between these ions and graphene oxide, like customary fluorescence -NPs e.g., semiconducting quantum dots. The fluorescence quenching owing to electron transfer among heavy metal ions and graphene oxide succeeding evolution of double-stranded DNA with a hairpin appearance that emerged after particular Hg2+/Ag+ ions adhering to single-stranded DNA (T-rich/C-rich) preceding its functionalization on the surface of graphene oxide [148,149]. For the manufacturing of nano compounds with dual radiance characteristics, semiconducting quantum dots were aided via GO displaying efficient fluorescence and were introduced by Li et al. [149]. It is advantageous in discriminative and responsive detection of Cu+ ions [149]. Advantageously, responsive and discriminative ascertainment of Cu+ ions has been achieved through observing its effect on NCs fluorescence red glow turning blue and red colored fluorescence emission of quantum dots got quenched while graphene oxide underwent no change in fluorescence shade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation between orthodox sensors and nano-sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Inorganic precursors with non-poisonous, cost-effective and bio-suitable in nature are used to prepare nanomaterials supported probs in contrast to traditionally employed probs. Virtually, such probes have been fascinated because of facile synthetic procedures and potential implications, inexpensiveness and increased LOD. For modelling and preparation of optical sensors, NMs are used as excellent translating-section due to their distinguishing characteristics. Furthermore, optical characteristics of NMs were arrayed by target-initiated aggregation/anti-aggregation and surface improvement [32,52]. In addition, optical sensors of nano substances were analyzed with smaller sized ligands/macro-biomolecules being the identification module. Particular and distinguished sensing of heavy metal ions was also possible with the detection section that furnishes the target specimen with unique biological and chemical interactions. Moreover, swift qualitative/quantitative sensing of heavy metals with greater selectivity in comparison to colorimetric detectors has been achieved using luminescence-based nanoparticles. On contrary, some of the disadvantages related to the aqueous detections and biologically active metal ions detection were noticed on the rounds of poor water solubility, toxicity and inappropriateness of orthodox photoluminescent organic dyes like organic fluorophores and emissive conjugated-polymers [150]. For apprehending organic luminescence, a detailed overview of the structure and synthetic pathways of the compound must be known in comparison to luminescent nanoparticles. Photobleaching of organic dyes could happen even with facile observation and elevated susceptibility. The photo-bleaching of organic chromophores is repressed by Raman sensors. However, electromagnetic amplification causes Raman scattering remarkably effective when poor efficiency was faced. Therefore, SERES and LOD could proficiently be improved with standardization of constitution, volume and configuration of plasmonic nano-structures [151].

Furthermore, the implementation of nanomaterials as sensing modalities has greatly improved the sensing abilities of chemosensors. In an aqueous solution, the distinctive identification of Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions were achieved through immobilization of emissive dipeptidyl chemosensor (DPN) over silicon nanoparticles generating SiNPs@DPN by Chung and his coworkers [91]. Conjugation of MSiNPs with DPN remarkably enhanced LOD up to 96 nM for Hg2+ ions. Metallic ions in lower amounts were ascribed to nanoparticles triggering signal enlargement. Likewise, 0.768 μM LOD was observed for discerning Cu2+ ions under chemosensor radiant of rhodamine B [152]. Nevertheless, an upgraded LOD of less than 0.3 mg/L and boosted response for probing Cu2+ ions in the solution state was exhibited by surface modified MSNPs with rhodamine implanted through covalent bonds. Noticeable implications are discerned for lab on chip technology and bioimaging owing to efficient regenerative ability and resilience of hybrid-substance. Macro-biomolecules and micro-molecular ligands were employed probing agent for contrasting organic fluorescent nanosensors and chemosensors.

The necessity of unique tools for real-time detection of diverse water contaminants is generally recognized around the world. This review offered a critical appraisal of recent advances in a real-time monitoring system, based on the fluorescent, luminescence and colorimetric detection methods. The focus of this review ass to present the advancement in nanomaterial-based nanosensors for sensing heavy metal ions in aqueous matrices with enhanced selectivity, efficacy and sensitivity. Sensors exhibited better sensitivity and selectivity with the incorporation of nanomaterials. In a comparison of conventional sensors, nanomaterial-based sensors are found preferable because of facile synthesis, lower LOD values and cost-effectiveness in various practical applications. Despite significant advancements and developments, the recognition of heavy metal ions by these nanostructures still faces significant developmental problems related to their suitability in real-world samples such as biological and raw water samples. The weak self-stability of nanostructures must be addressed to meet the requirement for heavy metal ions monitoring in complex matrices such as biological samples, wastewater and seawater samples. As a result, new stable nanomaterials must be designed and developed, necessitating the use of novel modification techniques for enhanced functionalization. Additionally, in designing the nanomaterial-based sensors, selectivity must be addressed by selecting suitable binding sites such as specific biological macromolecules or small organic molecules which selectively binds the analytes of major concern. Finally, combining nano-systems with other technologies such as microfluidic or paper chips, as well as test strips, holds a lot of potential for the creation of portable devices that can detect heavy metal ions on a commercial or industrial scale. In conclusion, combining nano-systems with other technologies such as paper chips, microfluidic as well as test strips, holds a lot of potential for the creation of portable devices that can detect heavy metal ions on an industrial or commercial scale.

All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.
